common_name: Hosui Asian Pear (Japanese Golden-Russet Round Asian Pear)

planting.note: Plant bare-root trees in late winter while dormant; Hosui requires approximately 400–450 chilling hours (hours below 45°F) — achievable in most of zone 7–9 in most years. Asian pears are not self-fertile — plant at least two different Asian pear varieties (Shinsei, Twentieth Century, Olympic, or another Pyrus pyrifolia) within 50 feet for pollination; European pears (Pyrus communis) are generally not compatible pollinators. Hosui fruit ripens in August in zone 9 — earlier than most Asian pears — and should be harvested when still firm on the tree (unlike European pears, Asian pears do not improve in storage and should be harvested at full ripeness). Fruits do not ripen after harvest the way European pears do.

directions: Plant grafted nursery trees in late winter in full sun, well-drained soil; thin fruit clusters to one fruit per cluster when fruits are marble-sized for maximum size. Asian pears need no post-harvest ripening period. Hosui is a Japanese-bred Asian pear (nashi pear, Pyrus pyrifolia) — one of the finest Asian pear varieties available in North America for both flavor quality and adaptability to warmer zones (zone 5–9 with adequate chill). The fruit is large (8–12 oz), round to slightly oblate, with distinctive golden-brown russeted skin — the russet texture of Hosui's skin is one of its defining aesthetic characteristics, giving it a warm, autumn-toned appearance that differs from the smooth green or yellow skin of other Asian pear varieties. The flesh is the extraordinary feature: extremely crisp, almost crunchy — quite unlike the soft, butter-like texture of ripe European pears — with very high juice content and a sweet, aromatic, complex flavor with notes of honey and vanilla. Asian pears are eaten fresh at harvest, when crisp and juicy at peak quality; they do not improve with storage and are not used for cooking or canning the way European pears are. Hosui is among the most popular and widely planted Asian pear varieties in Japan and California, where its early ripening, excellent flavor, and moderate chill requirement make it adaptable to a wider range of climates than many Asian pear types.
sources: University of California Cooperative Extension — Asian Pear Variety Guide
Oregon State University Extension — Asian Pear Variety Comparison
California Rare Fruit Growers — Hosui Asian pear variety notes

common_name: Szukis American Persimmon (Large-Fruited Selected Native Persimmon Cultivar)

planting.note: Plant nursery-grown grafted trees in late winter or early spring; Szukis is self-fertile and produces without a cross-pollinator, though crop size may improve with a second American persimmon nearby. Plant in a permanent location — persimmons develop a deep taproot that makes transplanting difficult after establishment. In zone 9, Szukis ripens in September–October. American persimmons are astringent until fully ripe — do not harvest until the fruit is extremely soft (almost jelly-like) or until after a hard frost — premature consumption produces an intensely astringent, mouth-puckering effect from the tannins in underripe fruit.

directions: Plant grafted trees in well-drained soil in full sun or partial shade; American persimmons tolerate poor, dry, acidic soils that challenge most fruit trees. Allow fruit to ripen fully and become very soft before harvesting. Szukis is a selected cultivar of the American Persimmon (Diospyros virginiana) — a native North American tree with a natural range from Connecticut to Florida and west to Nebraska and Kansas, making it one of the most widely distributed native fruit trees in eastern North America. The American persimmon was an important food for Indigenous peoples and early European settlers, who ate the ripe fruit fresh, dried, and fermented into persimmon beer; persimmon pudding remains a traditional Appalachian and Midwestern dessert made from the pulp of ripe fruit. Szukis was selected from the wild by Larry Szukis for its exceptional fruit size for an American persimmon — the fruits can reach 1.5–2.5 oz, substantially larger than typical wild American persimmon fruits. The flavor of fully-ripe American persimmon is dramatically different from the popular Asian persimmons (Diospyros kaki) — more complex, intensely sweet, winey, and honey-like, with a jammy quality that many consider superior to the blander sweetness of the kaki types. As a native plant, Szukis/American persimmon provides exceptional wildlife value: the fruits are eaten by deer, raccoons, foxes, and over 40 bird species.
sources: Northern Nut Growers Association — American persimmon cultivar documentation
Kentucky State University Small Farms Program — American Persimmon Variety Trial
Lady Bird Johnson Wildflower Center — Diospyros virginiana native range and ecology

common_name: Pimiento de Padrón Pepper (Spanish Tapas Pepper, One-in-Ten Hot Novelty Pepper)

planting.note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before transplanting; Padrón peppers are harvested very young (1.5–2 inches long) at the green stage before any heat develops — the occasional hot fruit is random and appears even in young peppers. Harvest when fruits are small, bright green, and smooth; larger, older fruits are more likely to be hot. In zone 9, transplant March–April for spring and summer production. Padrón plants are prolific and compact (18–24 inch), making them well-suited to container growing and small gardens. The tradition of gambling on which pepper will be hot is a cherished element of eating Padrón peppers in Spanish culinary culture.

directions: Start seeds indoors at 78–85°F; transplant after last frost. Harvest peppers when small (1.5–2 inches), bright green, and tender. Cook whole in a screaming-hot cast iron skillet with olive oil and coarse salt — the classic preparation. Pimiento de Padrón (Padrón Pepper) is a small Spanish sweet frying pepper from the municipality of Padrón in Galicia, northwestern Spain — famous throughout Spain and now internationally for its unusual roulette-like characteristic: most fruits (roughly 9 in 10) are mild and sweet, but a small percentage of fruits within any given harvest are randomly and intensely hot (estimated 5,000–20,000 Scoville units). The heat distribution is random — you cannot visually distinguish hot from mild fruits, and the proportion of hot fruits increases as the season progresses and when plants are under stress. This unpredictability makes eating Padrón peppers a social ritual: they are placed in a bowl and eaten communally, with everyone alerting the group when they encounter the occasional hot pepper, providing a light gambling element to the meal. The Galician expression 'os pementos de Padrón, uns pican e outros non' ('Padrón peppers: some are hot, some are not') has become one of the most recognized food sayings in Spanish culture. The traditional preparation — whole peppers blistered in very hot olive oil and salted — is now a standard tapas preparation across Spain and in Spanish restaurants worldwide.
sources: Food and Agriculture Organization — Padrón pepper Galicia geographical indication documentation
Johnny's Selected Seeds — Padrón pepper variety notes
Spanish Ministry of Agriculture — Pimiento de Padrón denomination of origin

common_name: Thai Bird Chili (Bird's Eye Chile, Tiny Ultra-Hot SE Asian Cooking Pepper)

planting.note: Start indoors 8–10 weeks before transplanting; Thai bird chili requires high soil temperature (80–95°F) for germination — use a heat mat and be patient, as germination can be slow (14–21 days). In zone 9, transplant March–April; Capsicum frutescens is actually a short-lived tropical perennial that may overwinter in zone 9b–10 in protected locations, potentially producing for 2–3 seasons. Plants become highly productive in Gulf Coast summers, setting hundreds of small peppers per plant. Harvest green for a fresher, slightly less hot flavor or allow to ripen red for maximum heat and a fruitier note. One plant produces more peppers than most households can use.

directions: Start seeds on heat mat at 85–90°F; transplant to full sun after last frost. A single plant produces abundantly — dry excess peppers for storage. Thai Bird Chili (Bird's Eye Chile, Phrik Khi Nu in Thai) is the foundational hot pepper of Thai, Vietnamese, and Southeast Asian cuisines — small (0.5–1 inch), thin, pointed, upright peppers that measure 50,000–100,000 Scoville heat units, making them significantly hotter than jalapeños (8,000 SHU) and approaching the lower range of habaneros. The name 'Bird's Eye' or 'Bird Chile' refers to the upright-pointing, small fruit that resembles a bird's eye, and also to the belief that birds (which lack capsaicin receptors) eat the peppers and spread seeds — a genuine ecological relationship that explains why the plant produces small, highly visible peppers. Thai Bird Chili is not a single variety but a group of similar Capsicum frutescens and Capsicum annuum types grown across Southeast Asia under various local names. The peppers are used in Thai cooking in virtually every form of heat addition — fresh sliced in curries and soups, dried and ground in chili pastes, pickled, or dried whole for fish sauce preparations. For Gulf South gardeners, Thai Bird Chili is one of the most productive and low-maintenance hot peppers in the summer garden: the plant thrives in heat and humidity that challenges most other pepper types, setting fruit prolifically even in July-August conditions that cause many pepper varieties to drop flowers.
sources: Johnny's Selected Seeds — Thai Bird Chili variety notes
Kitazawa Seed Company — Southeast Asian hot pepper varieties
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Hot Pepper Production for Florida and Gulf South

common_name: Peredovik Black Oil Sunflower (Russian High-Oil Bird-Feeding and Oil-Production Sunflower)

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost; Peredovik is a multi-stemmed, multi-head oilseed sunflower producing many 3–5 inch flower heads per plant (unlike the single giant-headed ornamental sunflowers grown for show). In zone 9, plant March–May; for fall dove season, plant July–August to time seed maturity with the September–October dove hunting season. The oil sunflower habit (shorter, multi-branching, many small heads) maximizes seed yield per plant and provides more uniform ripening than single-headed types. Harvest when seed heads are thoroughly dry and birds begin eating the seeds.

directions: Direct sow 0.5–1 inch deep after last frost; thin to 12–18 inches. Harvest when seed heads are completely dry and brown. Peredovik (also spelled 'Peredovik') is a Russian open-pollinated oilseed sunflower developed in the Soviet Union specifically for oil production — one of the most widely planted sunflower varieties in the world and the standard variety used in commercial sunflower oil production globally before the development of modern high-oleic hybrids. The Peredovik breeding emphasized oil percentage over seed size: the thin, black seeds contain 42–45% oil by weight (compared to 24–28% for striped ornamental/snack sunflower types), and this high oil content is what gives 'black oil sunflower' its distinct value. In the bird-feeding context, black oil sunflower seeds (Peredovik or similar black-seeded varieties) are universally recommended as the premier wild bird food: the thin, easy-to-crack shell is accessible to a wider range of small songbirds than the thick-shelled striped types, and the high oil content provides superior energy density for birds — particularly important for overwintering and migratory species. For wildlife food plots in the Gulf South, Peredovik-type black oil sunflower is one of the primary dove and songbird attractants planted specifically for the September-October dove season. The seeds are also excellent for human consumption (roasting, pressing for oil) though small in size.
sources: USDA National Agricultural Library — Helianthus annuus oilseed variety documentation
Cornell Lab of Ornithology — Black oil sunflower as premier bird food
Russian Institute of Plant Industry — Peredovik sunflower variety development
